1.pericope_label: A New Song for the Coming Judge
1.synthesis: “Oh sing to the LORD a new song, for he has done marvelous things! His right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation” (v.1). The LORD has made known his salvation and remembered his steadfast love to Israel, so that “all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God” (vv.2–3). Let the sea roar, the rivers clap, the hills sing for joy “before the LORD, for he comes to judge the earth… with righteousness” (vv.7–9). The integrative thread is exuberant praise for God’s saving victory revealed to all the earth: the new song celebrates that God’s own arm has won salvation and made it known to the nations, summoning all creation to joyful worship of the coming righteous Judge.
1.historical_context: Psalm 98 (the “Cantate Domino”) celebrates God’s salvation accomplished by his “right hand and holy arm” (v.1), language echoing the Exodus (Exodus 15:6) and Isaiah’s vision of God baring his arm before the nations (Isaiah 52:10). The universal scope — “all the ends of the earth” seeing salvation (v.3) — and creation’s joy (vv.7–8) shape it as an enthronement and salvation hymn.
1.literary_structure: The psalm divides into praise for past salvation (vv.1–3), the call to universal praise with instruments (vv.4–6), and creation’s joy before the coming Judge (vv.7–9). The “new song” (v.1) and the coming to judge (v.9) bracket it with the themes of salvation accomplished and consummated.
1.theological_application: God’s salvation is his own achievement — “his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation” (v.1) — and is meant to be seen by “all the ends of the earth” (v.3; Luke 3:6). Such salvation calls for a “new song” of exuberant, whole-creation praise (v.4). The believer rejoices that redemption is God’s work, not ours, and joins the cosmic anticipation of the righteous Judge whose coming will set the world right (2 Timothy 4:8). God’s salvation is his own achievement — “his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation” (v.1) — and is meant to be seen by “all the ends of the earth” (v.3; Luke 3:6). Such salvation calls for a “new song” of exuberant, whole-creation praise (v.4). The believer rejoices that redemption is God’s work, not ours, and joins the cosmic anticipation of the righteous Judge whose coming will set the whole world right (2 Timothy 4:8).
1.christology: The salvation worked by God’s “holy arm” and seen by all the earth (v.1, v.3) is fulfilled in Christ, the arm of the LORD revealed (Isaiah 53:1; John 12:38), whose salvation Simeon beheld as “a light for revelation to the Gentiles” (Luke 2:30–32). This psalm’s themes echo in Mary’s and Zechariah’s songs of the Savior (Luke 1:51, Luke 1:69). The “new song” for God’s victory is sung to the Lamb who by his blood won salvation for every nation (Revelation 5:9).
